, and spent eighteen years alone there before she was rescued. The main facts are as follows:
According to the information I have obtained from
through the interpreter, the history of
She was absent, gathering wood, when
were taken away, but returned to the camp or quarters, and finding them deserted, she followed in time to be taken aboard
about 3 years old, she plunged into the water and swam ashore in search of them.
, she returned to the beach just in time to see
, but the only reply she got, and which she remembered to the day of her death, was "manyana," pronounced mah-nyah-nah, the Spanish word for "to-morrow," evidently meaning that
would return for her to-morrow or the following day. She threw herself down on the beach and cried long and bitterly. She did not find
, and supposed they were either taken off by
or carried away and devoured by the wild dogs on
. She became very sick and lay a long time without either water or food, but finally recovered and forgot her grief in wandering about

. She lived on a plant resembling the cabbage, called by Californians "palosanto," and a root known by the name of "corcomite;" also a yellow root, the name of which was not given, and seal or sea-lion blubber. As she had abalone shellfish hooks, and lines made of the sinews of the seal, it is probable she supplied herself with fish from the ocean.
An approaching storm and night coming on, the water being quite shoal about
and unsafe for a vessel during a high wind, they were compelled to leave for the safety of
, intending to return at a future time for
ran before the gale and reached
but circumstances did not permit a return prior to the loss of
in a storm a few weeks later, and the distance to
was too great to be safely made in the small shore boats.

, otter hunting, and saw signs of a human inhabitant. He saw a footprint made during the previous rainy season, sunken deeply in the now dry, hard ground, and from the size judged it to be that of a woman; also three small circular inclosures about 200 yards from the beach, and about a mile apart. They were about six feet in diameter, made of brush; the walls five feet high, with a small opening on one side. Near these openings were sticks of driftwood, stuck in the ground in the form of a tripod, supporting dried seal blubber. These inclosures appeared to be simply wind breaks, affording no protection from the rain. The investigation was pursued no further, as an approaching storm compelled him to leave
having seen many otter on his first visit to
, made a second during the winter of 1852, and, being requested by the
, had determined to make a careful hunt for
the supposed lone inhabitant of the island
being better supplied with fresh water closer to the beach and better facilities for fishing, and the altitude such as to command a view of the greater portion of
, he supposed if alive she would be found there, and made his arrangements accordingly. He and

found the same or similar inclosure above mentioned, and the tripod sticks supporting renewed pieces of blubber. In the neighborhood of the huts or wind-breaks were seven or eight wild dogs, about the size of a coyote (a small wolf on the coast), and in color black and white.
said he had seen the same kind of dogs among the Northwestern Indians. They appeared to be very wild. Within half a mile of the head of
they discovered a basket in the crotch of a bush or small tree, covered with a seal-skin, and containing a dress made of shags' skins (the shag is a species of duck on

that can neither walk nor fly), carefully folded up, and several square pieces of skins similar to those of which the dress was made; also a rope made of seal sinews, abalone shell-fish hooks, bone needles, etc. As it was late and time for them to return to
scattered the contents of the basket on the ground, so that upon his return he could judge of the presence or absence of
, by finding them gathered up or remaining as he left them. The following four or more days were busily spent in otter hunting, and before the search for
was renewed a southeast gale compelled them to seek a more hospitable harbor at the
in July, 1853, and although otter hunting was the main object of the visit,
was not forgotten. They landed on the northeast side of
early in the day, and, having selected their camping site, all but one,

. Near there were observed fresh footprints leading from the beach to the ridge or higher elevation, but were soon lost in moss-covered ground. The following day they were more successful. One of the party,
at a distance, and cautiously approach in an opposite direction from the

, got quite close to her without being observed. She was in one of her pens, or wind-breaks, clothed in a garment made of the skins of a shag, without sleeves, low necked, and as observed when standing up, extending almost to the ankles. She was sitting cross-legged, skinning seal blubber with a rude knife, made of a piece of hoop iron driven into a piece of wood.

There was no covering on her head excepting a thick mass of matted hair of a yellowish brown color, due to the exposure to the sun and air. The hair was short, looking as though the free ends had rotted off. She would occasionally raise her hand and shade her eyes and look toward
on a sandy plain near the beach, whom she evidently saw. There were two or three of the wild dogs around the inclosure, who began to growl as
approached, but ran off at the bidding of

were now signalled in order that she might be captured if she attempted to escape. To the surprise of all she made no attempt to get away, but greeted each one as they approached with a bow and a smile, and chatted all the time in a dialect none of them understood, although
were acquainted with several Indian dialects. She was talking, apparently to herself, from the time
approached within hearing until she was made aware of his presence.

Within the inclosure was a fire smoldering, and a large ash pile outside showed that to have been her abiding place for some time. She was very hospitable, preparing a meal from her limited store of roots before named, and serving
with a grace and bearing that surprised them all.

The expression of her face was pleasing, her features were regular, and her complexion much fairer, and her form more symmetrical than that of the Indian women on the main land, and she is believed to have belonged to
, which opinion is strengthened by the fact that
, a Frenchman, now engaged in collecting archæological and other specimens of bygone years on the
similar to those previously collected in
, and further north, and unlike anything found on either
. Also the skulls and other bones of the human skeleton found there are unlike those found in other sections of
. A notable observation is the absence of any evidence of syphilis, a striking contrast with other human bones found in this latitude.
I have had the privilege of examining several skulls exhumed from
, showing very plainly the ravages of this disease in those who have passed away probably centuries ago.

Near her inclosure were several stakes, between which was stretched a rope made of the seal sinews, supporting seal and sea elephant blubber. And further on were three huts made of whale's ribs, covered with brush, but giving no evidence of having been lately occupied. By signs and other means of communication she was made aware that they wanted her to accompany them, and, without any apparent hesitation, she made ready to follow. She filled a basket—such as were common among the Indians of this coast for packing on their backs—with provisions, fishing tackle, and other articles, and, taking it on one arm and a firebrand in the other hand, she followed

lay at anchor they found a beautiful spring of water issuing from the bank above the beach, under a shelving rock. The cracks or fissures in this rock were stuck full of bones, and there were other evidences of an encampment of

. These bones were used for nourishment, obtained by sucking; they were dried and resucked many times, showing that occasionally she was put upon short rations, but at the time of her discovery appeared to have an abundance, such as it was. She retained all her teeth, but they were worn low, supposed to be due to her chewing tough and solid articles of food. Her age appeared to be about 50 years.
Further on was another spring near the beach, under the bank. Here she indicated a desire to bathe, and was permitted to do so by the
retiring out of sight. After her bath she joined
and went direct to the shore boat and thence to
. She was prevailed upon to abandon her firebrand and seal blubber before leaving the beach. Going aboard
, she went directly to the stove and warmed herself, indicating that she knew what the stove was for. She ate heartily of the food of
, appeared to enjoy it, and it agreed with her.

made her a skirt of ticking, with which, and a sailor's cotton shirt and a black necktie, her dress was complete. She assisted in making her dress, but could not see to thread her necktie [needle]. Her sewing was quite rude. She appeared much pleased with her new dress.
The following day she went ashore with
, who made a separate shelter for her, which she occupied about a month, the time spent on
otter hunting. She was quite industrious, carrying wood and water without being asked.
She appeared contented and happy, frequently singing and chattering to herself.
could not understand what she said, nor she they, but
said it was truly wonderful how she could converse with them by signs.
She occupied a portion of her time in making water vessels of grass and asphaltum, a substance plentiful both on

. The grass was plaited into a shape resembling a demijohn, but wider at the top, and lined with asphaltum. She would put in several small pieces of the last-mentioned substance, and then drop on them small stones or pebbles about the size of hulled walnuts, heated sufficiently to melt the asphaltum, and, grasping the neck with both hands, the lower end on the ground, give it a rapid rotary motion for several minutes, and then empty the stones on the ground. The vessels had a continuous coating on the inside, and are reported to have been water-tight, the lining even resisting the heat of the sun when covered with water. She had both stone and earthen vessels in which to boil water and cook if desired. To procure seal blubber she could kill the seals with a club when on land sunning themselves or snare them with her sinewy ropes. She obtained fire by rubbing two dry sticks together. One of them, partly flat, had a groove along its entire length, the other was pointed, and fitted into the groove, in which it was rubbed rapidly backward and forward until it burned.

, soon found themselves at sea in a storm. She made signs that she could stop the storm, and, obtaining permission, she knelt on the deck facing the quarter whence the wind came and commenced muttering something supposed to be a prayer.
She soon got up, but continued the prayer at intervals during the day, apparently without fear, and when the wind began to abate she turned to
and, with a smile, made signs that her prayers had been answered.
When they came in sight of
, she told them by signs that it was an island, and indicated from there how she had seen from
ships pass up and down, but never land. Also that she had seen the
the day previous to her discovery as they passed to the head of
. She was afraid to make herself known until she knew that there was no danger to her person.
There were many places of concealment on
, and unless taken unawares, she would have been hard to find.
As she was about to land at
an ox team passed which so delighted her that she talked, laughed, danced and gesticulated, and before that excitement ended

approached that gave her even more pleasure than the appearance of the ox team. At first it was supposed that she thought the man and horse constituted one animal, but, if so, the mistake was soon corrected, for on landing she went up to the horse and carefully examined it. The examination gave her additional pleasure; she would turn to
and, laughing, request them to look at the horse. She straddled the thumb of the left hand by the two forefingers of the right, and moved them to represent the horse and
as he passed along the beach.
, where she became the center of attraction. The
took a great interest in her, sending to
and other places, hoping to find some one who could converse with her, but failed. Even the
on the island, could not understand her.
became very very much attached to her, and, although
offered largely for the privilege of taking her to
and placing her on exhibition, it was refused; also, an offer of $1,000 from
for a like purpose was refused.

Many people called on her, and she received them kindly, but the afternoon was her favorite time for receiving calls, when she would put on her shag-skin dress and entertain her visitors with a song-dance. She cared nothing for money, giving it to the children when given to her. She did not wish to sleep in a bed, did not desire bed covering, but was careful not to expose her person. Her manners were not rude, and in many things she was much more refined than many who enjoy civilized privileges, yet in many things she was very much like a child. She wanted everything that she saw that appeared pleasant to the eye or taste, and if fruit was withheld from her she would plead for it in such a child-like manner that it was hard to refuse her.

When found she was in excellent physical condition, strong and active; but the eating of fruit and vegetables brought on a diarrhæa or dysentery in about three weeks after she landed, and that, in connection with an injury in the spine, received by falling from a porch, terminated her life four weeks later, or seven weeks from the time she landed. During her sickness she reluctantly permitted

to dress her in flannel underclothes, and took her bed under proper covering, but positively refused to return to her former plain diet, as was proposed by some who called to see her. Her dress of shag skins, basket and trinkets were given to
, who, it is said, sent them to
